items.title: Albanese poised to kill off move to increase taxes on gas giants

children.content: The government will not increase taxes on gas giants in next month's budget, echoing the industry's argument that exporters are already paying tens of billions of dollars in taxes each year.

articles.title: Research for Congress raises doubts over AUKUS submarines

items.title: 'Sad for us': Hannah Clarke's parents step back from advocacy

children.content: After their daughter and three grandchildren were murdered, Sue and Lloyd Clarke began a charity to improve awareness of coercive control. Now they've decided they need to step back for their own health.

image.altText: Hannah Clarke, hugs her son Trey Baxter, while standing on a beach.

items.title: Documents reveal fresh details about Roberts-Smith’s overseas moving plans

children.content: Police were concerned war veteran Ben Roberts-Smith was attempting to move overseas and possibly to Singapore or Spain, court documents reveal.

image.altText: Ben Roberts-Smith looks at the camera outside a court building

items.title: Trump orders US to 'shoot and ‌kill any ​boat' laying mines in ​the ‌Strait of ⁠Hormuz

children.content: Donald Trump says he has ordered the US Navy to "shoot and kill any boat" laying mines in Strait of Hormuz.

items.title: The battle of the blockades in the Strait of Hormuz

image.altText: Masked men in sand-coloured army fatigues climbing a ladder off the side of a ship.

children.content: As part of its blockade of Iran, ships have been seized by US forces, others fired upon by Iran and, most recently, boarded by the regime's revolutionary guards. This is all while both sides maintain there is a ceasefire.

items.title: Three children attacked by dingoes in national park

children.content: Three children have been attacked by dingoes in Karijini National Park since Saturday, prompting a warning from authorities.

image.altText: A dingo howling towards the camera

items.title: Canavan's frosty foray into Farrer's campaign trail

children.content: This week the Albanese government pushed out a major reform ahead of the federal budget, the Nationals leader was out in the cold on the campaign trail and a former Treasury secretary had some choice words about the push for gas tax reform.

image.altText: Matt Canavan farrer

items.title: Now James Valentine is gone, I'll let you in on the secret of his success

children.content: Children grew up with him, adults grew old with him, he kept us connected to our city, the world and each other. James helped us make sense of life and gave us permission to laugh at the absurdity of it all — and ourselves.

articles.title: James Valentine swapped microphones — and found a new audience who loved him

children.content: James Valentine started his career on the stage, but it was his time behind the microphone in the studio and an interactive style that came to define him.

image.altText: a man smiles while holding his chin

articles.title: James Valentine, beloved ABC talkback radio star, dies aged 64

children.content: The presenter and professional saxophone player is being remembered for his ability to explore the humour and oddities of daily life on the airwaves.

image.altText: a man sitting down smiling while clasping his hands together

gallery.altText: James Valentine, wearing a read jumper and black headphones, smiling and talking into a microphone.

items.title: How the new eligibility test for NDIS participants could work

image.altText: A close up of a man's hands in his lap.

children.content: Labor's plan to create a new "assessment tool" to determine future NDIS eligibility based on functional need could backfire without the right settings, say experts.

items.title: Award-winning author who put Brisbane on the literary map dies aged 92

children.content: Erudite and urbane, David Malouf — perhaps most famous for his portrait of mid-century Brisbane in his 1975 novel Johnno — will be fondly remembered for his immeasurable contribution to Australian letters.

image.altText: Author David Malouf.

children.content: Cyber criminals have hacked Sri Lanka's finance ministry, stealing millions of dollars destined to repay Australia.

children.content: Scientists say they have the first direct evidence that seeds can sense the sound of rain, and will sprout faster when they do.

image.altText: Green seedling shoots covered in tiny water droplets in dark soil.

items.title: Sheraz risked his life translating in Afghanistan. His family paid the price

children.content: Sheraz worked with Australian and US troops in Afghanistan, as a result his family were targeted by the Taliban. This Anzac Day he's being heroed.

items.title: War on hoons

children.content: You might have heard of hooning – in which people — usually young men — drift, race, and do burnouts on public roads.
It happens across the country and has been around for decades. But, it’s getting bigger, louder and more visible.
So are police crackdowns. Fines, car seizures and jail terms are all on the cards for people caught hooning.
Despite the laws and serious dangers, people still do it. But, why?

image.altText: Blue backgroun with white text. Text reads Inside Australia's illegal drift racing world. ABC News Loop logo

gallery.altText: Blue backgroun with white text. Text reads Inside Australia's illegal drift racing world. ABC News Loop logo

thumbnail.altText: Blue backgroun with white text. Text reads Inside Australia's illegal drift racing world. ABC News Loop logo

items.title: New grapevine virus GINV detected for first time in Australia
